topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of a dropped stone. *Party cancelled.* My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping near my temple. I reread the subject line twice, half expecting a prank, the other half bracing for the disappointment. I didn't move from the sofa. My hands, resting on the worn fabric, felt strangely heavy, rooted to the spot. A slow, deliberate exhale escaped me, a puff of air that didn't quite clear the tightness in my chest. I stood up, walked to the window, and stared out at the indifferent streetlights. Why? It simply didn't compute. I picked up my phone, intending to call the organizer, but the words wouldn't form. I just stared at the screen, my thumb hovering over the dial button, a stubborn refusal to accept the reality settling deep in my core.

[story 2]

"Are you serious?" I managed, the words sounding brittle even to my own ears. The silence in the room was thick, amplifying the sting of the news. I didn't look at Sarah; I focused instead on the intricate pattern of the rug beneath my feet, tracing the fibers with my eyes as if the pattern held the answer. A low hum of frustration vibrated through my jaw. I paced the length of the living room, a tight, jerky motion. Each step was weighted with refusal. I wanted to argue, to demand an explanation that made sense, but the energy to articulate the sheer absurdity of the situation seemed to have evaporated. I stopped abruptly by the bookshelf, running a hand over the spines of old novels, my knuckles white against the wood. This wasn't over. I wasn't going to let this deflate me.

[story 3]

I let the silence stretch, letting the news sit there, unacknowledged. My lips pressed into a thin, hard line. The initial shock had curdled into a simmering resentment. I walked into the kitchen, the clink of ceramic mugs sounding offensively cheerful in the quiet. I poured myself a glass of water, the ice rattling loudly in the glass—a small, sharp sound that seemed to match the irritation bubbling beneath my skin. I didn't look toward the door where the party was supposed to be happening. Instead, I focused intently on washing the dishes, scrubbing the plates with unnecessary vigor. It was a pointless exercise, this denial, I knew, but the physical act of cleaning felt like a necessary countermeasure against the sinking feeling in my stomach. I finished the last plate with a decisive scrape, turning on the tap, determined to keep moving forward, regardless of the empty space where celebration should have been.
